note_data.title: Selection and evolution
keyPoints: **Natural selection** acts on phenotypes, causing differential survival and reproduction of organisms best adapted to their environment
**Directional selection** shifts the mean of a population in one direction, **stabilising selection** reduces variation around the mean, **disruptive selection** favours extreme phenotypes
**Speciation** occurs when populations become reproductively isolated and genetic differences accumulate until interbreeding is no longer possible
**Allopatric speciation** results from geographical separation, **sympatric speciation** occurs without geographical barriers through mechanisms like polyploidy
**Hardy-Weinberg principle** states that allele frequencies remain constant in a population when there is no mutation, selection, migration, or non-random mating
**Genetic drift** causes random changes in allele frequencies, with greater effects in smaller populations leading to loss of genetic diversity
**Founder effect** occurs when a small group establishes a new population with reduced genetic variation compared to the original population
**Bottleneck effect** dramatically reduces population size, causing loss of alleles and reduced genetic diversity in surviving individuals

definitions.definition: The differential survival and reproduction of organisms with favourable characteristics that are inherited, leading to evolutionary change
The formation of new species when populations become reproductively isolated and can no longer interbreed to produce fertile offspring
Random changes in allele frequencies in a population, particularly significant in small populations
The total number of alleles of all genes in a breeding population at a given time
The prevention of gene flow between populations due to barriers that prevent successful interbreeding

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
State clear differences between two concepts using 'whereas' or 'while'
State what happens in logical sequence without explaining why

modelAnswer.question: Explain how the founder effect can lead to speciation. [3]
modelAnswer.answer: A small group of individuals becomes separated from the main population and establishes a new population. This founding population has a reduced gene pool/genetic diversity compared to the original population. Over time, different mutations arise and different selection pressures act on the two populations, causing genetic divergence until reproductive isolation occurs.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Small group separated/establishes new population
Mark 2: Reduced genetic diversity/gene pool in founding population
Mark 3: Genetic divergence leads to reproductive isolation/speciation

commonMistakes: Never say 'survival of the fittest' — always say 'differential survival and reproduction of organisms with favourable characteristics'
Students confuse genetic drift with natural selection — drift is random, selection is non-random based on fitness
Don't say organisms 'evolve to survive' — evolution has no direction or purpose, beneficial mutations are selected for
Never say 'acquired characteristics are inherited' — only genetic changes can be passed to offspring
Students think Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium is common in nature — it only occurs under very specific unrealistic conditions
examinerTips: This topic appears almost every year in Paper 2, especially Hardy-Weinberg calculations
Always specify the type of selection (directional/stabilising/disruptive) when describing natural selection
For speciation questions, clearly distinguish between allopatric and sympatric mechanisms
Hardy-Weinberg questions often involve percentage calculations — show your working clearly
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Graph showing normal distribution curves for directional, stabilising and disruptive selection, with arrows indicating direction of selection pressure and resulting population changes over time
